issue_title: Can anybody please share a working example of Server Side Encryption using Customer Provided Encryption Keys.
issue_body:
Please don't think that I am lazy. I am just tired of trying almost everything and not getting my code to work.

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/37844349/what-is-the-right-way-to-create-a-ssecustomerkey-for-boto-file-encryption-in-pyt

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/37833243/boto3-client-error-server-side-encryption-with-customer-provided-key-is-incompa

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/37247975/what-is-the-correct-way-of-passing-s3-headers-inside-django

Please can somebody point out what am I doing wrong in this code.

```
password = "32characterslongpassphraseneeded".encode('utf-8')
encryption_key = hashlib.md5(password).hexdigest()
encryption_key_md5 = hashlib.md5(encryption_key.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()
import boto3
s3 = boto3.resource('s3')
key = s3.Object(bucket_name, key_name)
kwargs = {
            'SSECustomerAlgorithm': 'AES256',
            'SSECustomerKey': encryption_key,
            'SSECustomerKeyMD5': encryption_key_md5,
            'ContentType': file_obj.content_type,
            'Body': file_obj,
        }


key.put(**kwargs)

```
I am getting the following error.

> An error occurred (InvalidArgument) when calling the PutObject operation: The calculated MD5 hash of the key did not match the hash that was provided.


I am very new to boto3 and and S3 API. I might be doing something very stupid but how I don't know how to fix this.

I am utilizing the same s3 api through a php client and it works fine.

```
$name="somename"
$customerKey = md5($name);
                    $s3->putObject([
                        'Bucket' => S3_BUCKET,
                        'Key'    => "scope/{$name}",
                        'Body'   => fopen($tmp_file_path, 'rb'),
                        'ACL'    => S3_ACL,
                        'SSECustomerAlgorithm' => 'AES256',
                        'SSECustomerKey'       => $customerKey,
                        'SSECustomerKeyMD5'    => md5($customerKey ,true),
                    ]);
```
The only difference I see here is that php's md5 method can take second argument which, if true, returns and 16 character long digest as compared to normal 32 character long digest. Now I don't know how to create a 16 character long digest using hashlib.md5.

I can't find any reference to S3 "calling format" in boto3.  At first I thought it was just a documentation oversight, but I can't find anything like that in the source code, either (though the source code is completely different from boto2, so maybe I just don't know what to look for).

Is this feature gone?  Without `OrdinaryCallingFormat`, is it now impossible to use this with [fake-s3](https://github.com/jubos/fake-s3)?

I found [this page](http://pokerwoles.com/____________overviewi_m_trying_to_overwrite_certain_variables_in_boto3_using_configuration_file/question_detail/32618216/overwrite_s3_endpoint_using_boto3_configuration_file) which suggests this will help:

    from botocore.utils import fix_s3_host
    resource.meta.client.meta.events.unregister('before-sign.s3', fix_s3_host)

but it doesn't seem to make a difference.

Here's the simplified test case I'm working with:

    import boto3
    from StringIO import StringIO
    session = boto3.session.Session(aws_access_key_id='1',
                                    aws_secret_access_key='2',
                                    region_name='3')
    s3 = session.resource('s3', endpoint_url='http://localhost:5353', use_ssl=False)
    bucket = s3.Bucket('my-local-bucket')

    obj = bucket.Object(key='my-test-key')
    obj.put(StringIO("abc"))

    obj = bucket.Object(key='my-test-key')
    obj.get()['Body'].read()  # returns the empty string

The documentation doesn't say, but empirically, it seems the keys passed to Session can't be None, but they can be any string.

I'm not sure whether this is a bug, a problem with the docs, or something I'm doing wrong, but I'm stuck and need guidance.

The code below corresponds to what's in the documentation [here](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/application-autoscaling.html#ApplicationAutoScaling.Client.put_scaling_policy):

```
def auto_scale_dynamodb(**kwargs):
    client = boto3.client('application-autoscaling')
    policy = kwargs['table_name'] + '-auto-scaling'
    resource = 'table/' + kwargs['table_name']

    response = client.put_scaling_policy(
        PolicyName=policy,
        ServiceNamespace='dynamodb',
        ResourceId=resource,
        ScalableDimension='dynamodb:table:ReadCapacityUnits',
        PolicyType='TargetTrackingScaling',
        TargetTrackingScalingPolicyConfiguration={
            'TargetValue' : 30000,
            'PredefinedMetricSpecification' : {
                'PredefinedMetricType' : 'DynamoDBReadCapacityUtilization'
            },
            'ScaleOutCooldown' : 30,
            'ScaleInCooldown' : 30
        }
    )
    return
```

But it throws this error: 
"Unknown parameter in input: "TargetTrackingScalingPolicyConfiguration", must be one of: PolicyName, ServiceNamespace, ResourceId, ScalableDimension, PolicyType, StepScalingPolicyConfiguration"

The [API docs](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/ApplicationAutoScaling/latest/APIReference/API_PutScalingPolicy.html) seem to agree with the boto3 docs, so I'm not sure what's up.

I've googled around, and found nothing. Suggestions?

client.posts('staff.tumblr.com', reblog_info=True)
```

In practice I have to do this:

``` python
client.posts('staff.tumblr.com', reblog_info='true')
```

because pytumblr adds uppercase boolean values as GET parameters ('True'), which the Tumblr API ignores.

Continued from #308

Any possibility this can be moved to MIT?  The ruby plugin is MIT.

If the issue is real world ramification I just had to rebase stuff out of a repo due to finding a worker copied LGPL code in.  I doubt I'm the only person affected by this.

issue_title: new functionality to re-use existing browser session
issue_body:
What do you guys think about this kind of enhancement:
Add a new KW (or extend the `Create Webdriver`) for attaching to an existing browser/selenium session.

This doesn't make that much sense in "production" environment but would be very useful when implementing test cases. The idea would be to speed-up following scenarios
-  When testing web apps there's quite often a scenario where you do lots of stuff and then something breaks: e.g. you have typo in your xpath, a timing issue with (e.g. jQuery not finished) or something else. When this happens you have to start from the beginning but it would be great if you could continue from the point where the TC broke ...
- In environments where the set-up is time consuming, it would great to reuse the browser session(s). For example a set-up where you open a browser and login to the web-app, open another browser and login to the CRM, open yet another browser and login to a web-email app. It would be great to keep those 3 browsers open and skip the browser opening and login steps...

I need this functionality and will implement it for sure, the question is just: should I implement it in my libraries (that use selenium2lib) or should I create a PR for this? 

Here a short example how to connect to an existing session:
- Open new browser, print out the session details and leave the browser open
````
> python sel.py 
c1c31e1e-9b19-4363-9ab7-369e84313601  http://127.0.0.1:55486/hub
````
- Go manually to google.com with the browser
- re-attach (with session details) and click the I'm feeling lucky button:
````
> python sel.py c1c31e1e-9b19-4363-9ab7-369e84313601  http://127.0.0.1:55486/hub
````


sel.py file:
````
from selenium.webdriver import Firefox, Remote
import sys

def open():
    driver = Firefox()
    print("%s  %s" % (driver.session_id, driver.command_executor._url))
    
def attach(sid, url):
    driver = Remote(command_executor=url, desired_capabilities={})
    driver.session_id = sid
    driver.find_element_by_xpath("//input[contains(@value, 'Lucky')]").click()

sid = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else None
url = sys.argv[2] if len(sys.argv) > 2 else None

if sid is None:
    open()
else:
    attach(sid, url)
````

issue_title: Pipenv fails to install Python 3.6.6 with pyenv
issue_body:
##### Issue description

I installed pipenv and pyenv via homebrew on Mac OS X 10.13.4.
I added the following line to my `~/.bash_profile`

    export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"
   
My Pipfile restricts the version of Python to 3.6

    [requires]
    python_version = "3.6"

I run 

    pipenv install --dev

##### Expected result

I expect pipenv to install the latest version of Python 3.6 via pyenv

```

When I do

```
$ pyenv install --list | grep 3.6
  3.3.6

  3.6.1
  3.6.2
  3.6.3

```
one can see, that a version `3.6.6` indeed doesn't exist, only a version `3.6.6rc1`. I think pipenv should try to install `3.6.5` and not `3.6.6` and also not `3.6.6rc1` (because that is an unstable version).

I am not sure whether this is more of an issue with Pipenv or the package I am trying to install, but I figure the chances are high other packages could have similar issues.

The package in question [numpy-quaternion](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/numpy-quaternion) has two 'versions' on PyPI, (note the extra zero in the second)
- `numpy-quaternion 2017.11.26.19.0.13`     (wheels)
- `numpy-quaternion 2017.11.26.19.00.13`   (source)

issue_title: While installing valid package, another package name is suggested.
issue_body:
I was trying to install [pymc3](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pymc3) with pipenv. pipenv asked me whether I wanted to install `pymc` instead.

```
 % pipenv install pymc3
Did you mean pymc? [Y/n]: n
Installing pymc3…
Collecting pymc3
  Downloading pymc3-3.2-py3-none-any.whl (1.2MB)
```

I don't know if this is the intended functionality of pipenv. I can very well imagine that this is intended and meant to reduce exploitation of typos in package names. Nevertheless, in such an instance I would find it much petter if pipenv mentioned to me that it actually found pymc3, but is suggesting another package name.

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type
MacOS (High Sierra)
1. Python version: `$ python -V`
Python 3.6.4
1. Pipenv version: `$ pipenv --version`
pipenv, version 8.3.1

##### Expected result

Either direct installation of `pymc3`, or if it is intended that asuggestion for another package is shown, a reference telling us that pymc3 exists.

issue_title: prevent private [[sources]] from showing up in Pipfile(s)
issue_body:
@techalchemy I've been using pipenv for months using `PIP_EXTRA_INDEX_URL` to point to our own private packages in our own index. 

pipenv never added this url to the Pipfile, but i've noticed it recently has started to. Not sure if this has been due to some update to `pipenv` or due to me switching to `pip.conf` files (instead of using environment variables--see below) to specify the `extra-index-url`. 

This is not ideal, because this url contains a "secret" token and so should ideally not be on version control (it'd be nice to avoid using yet another tool, e.g. `git-crypt` to deal with this).

Any ideas/suggestions?

## Why `pip.conf`?

The reason I had to switch to `pip.conf` was that `pip`/`pipenv` suddenly stopped "respecting" the `PIP_EXTRA_INDEX_URL` environtment variable. I realize this is likely outside the scope of `pipenv`, since I can't reproduce this myself on different setups (i.e. this suddenly happened when I switched to ubuntu 18) but would appreciate any ideas/help on how to resolve it.

issue_body: During the migration to the declarative languages approach we marked some tests as skipped, because the nature of code in those places almost completely changed. Now, when the behavior of working with languages is more likely finalized, those skipped tests should be rewritten to test that new code is still working for input from old cases. Tests should be designed in a way described [here](https://github.com/scrapinghub/dateparser/issues/31#issuecomment-71035831).

Sometimes there are dates that don't have all the information to get the exact date. Like "December 21" or "Friday". We can assume either current year and week, or the one that is latest in the past.

To achieve that, instead of calling `parse` method in `dateutil_parse` function we would need to call for the `_parse` method of dateutil parser and then, when we have information on what parts are parsed (and depending on configuration), we either chose date that is current week/month/year or the last one seen.

I haven't had time to write up a proper pull request. 
In the meantime if someone wants to use these for checking on lag or listing consumer groups go ahead. Add the two classes below to your code and then

```
req_handler = self.connect(broker.host, broker.port, self.handler)
future = req_handler.request(ListGroupRequest())
res = future.get(ListGroupResponse)

for group in res.groups:
   print group
```

Please note that you need to connect to all brokers to get the consumer groups
Also Kafka doesn't show consumer groups that are currently not connected

```
class ListGroupRequest(Request):
    """A list group request

    Specification::

    ListGroupsRequest =>
    """
    @property
    def API_KEY(self):
        """API_KEY for this request, from the Kafka docs"""
        return 16

    def get_bytes(self):
        """Create a new list group request"""
        output = bytearray(self.HEADER_LEN)
        self._write_header(output)
        return output


class ListGroupResponse(Response):
    """A list group response

    Specification::

    ListGroupsResponse => ErrorCode Groups
      ErrorCode => int16
      Groups => [GroupId ProtocolType]
        GroupId => string
        ProtocolType => string
    """
    error = None
    groups = None

    def __init__(self, buff):
        """Deserialize into a new Response

        :param buff: Serialized message
        :type buff: :class:`bytearray`
        """
        fmt = 'h [SS]'
        response = struct_helpers.unpack_from(fmt, buff, 0)

        error, groups = response
        self.error = error
        self.groups = []
        for group_name,_ in groups:
            self.groups.append(group_name)
```

issue_title: Allow for a way to work with multiple tenants
issue_body:
Currently the credentials are based off the format imposed by `TACC-Cloud/agave-cli` which places a `current` and a `backup` json files under `AGAVE_CACHEDIR`. Each of the json files contains the credentials for a user to interact with only one tenant at a time.

I propose we use a `config.json` under `AGAVE_CACHEDIR` which stores all pertinent information for all tenants a user is interacting with. The config file should keep the credentials for all tenants and specify which tenants is currently being used.

issue_title: Fix interpolation when there are not four neighbours present
issue_body:
Currently, when doing the interpolation, the ``interpolate_bilinear_lonlat`` function in Cython searches for the four closest neighbours as if it was on a regular grid. However, in some places in the HEALPix grid, a given point is not surrounded by four cells, but three. The following example shows pixel index 653824 in a HEALPix grid with nside=256 in ring order, and its neighbours:

![debug](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/314716/31053864-216a5d98-a69e-11e7-9ace-a57c9296690d.png)

The cross indicates the point where I was trying to interpolate, and where I got NaN with the current function. The position of the cross is lon=0.0 lat=-41.730291566995895

We should figure out what the correct behavior should be in this case - healpy deals with this case as follows:

```python
In [4]: healpy.get_interp_weights(256, 0.0, -41.730291566995895, nest=False, lonlat=True)
Out[4]: 
(array([653824, 653825, 655871, 654848]),
 array([ 0.40037327,  0.        ,  0.29981337,  0.29981337]))
```

I guess this must be doing some kind of [barycentric interpolation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barycentric_coordinate_system#Interpolation_on_a_triangular_unstructured_grid)?

Currently if one of the four pixels to use for interpolation is -1 we just return NaN, but I think in that case we need to add a special case for 3-point interpolation. I *think* that this should then cover all cases.

issue_title: SP columns near the boundary has less neighbors
issue_body:
@mrcslws 
When topology is turned on for spatial pooler, columns near the boundary has less number of columns in their "neighborhood", yet they share the same inhibitionRadius as other columns. 

The issue is whether we should enforce each SP column to have the same number of neighbors, a simple fix could be to replace `topology.neighborhood` with `topology.wrappingNeighborhood` on https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1508

Practically, SP columns preferentially form more connections to inputs near the boundary. To illustrate the problem, I plotted the number of SP columns that are connected to each input bits after training. The training dataset is uniformly distributed, but inputs near the corner/boundary are preferentially connected by SP columns.

![trauma_boosting_with_topology_epoch_149](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5067931/18728702/4700ce06-8003-11e6-9026-5e11b6edacca.png)

Just a small oversight (someone probably forgot to come back to rename it)...

[nupic/tests/unit/nupic/support/group_by_test.py](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/tests/unit/nupic/support/group_by_test.py#L27) has a main test class named ```ConnectionsTest``` which should be named GroupByTest. The inner class has no association with the ```Connections``` class, and specifically tests the new ```group_by2``` functionality.

Currently storage methods take a ``cliquet.Resource`` object as first parameter and are not very low level.

I suggest that we refactor/change this to allow low-level create/update/delete of ``records(str, str, json)``.

```python
request.storage.create('__history__', '__internal__', {"action": "click"})
```

```python
request.storage.create('__schema:' + collection, userid, {"title": "form"})
```

We have several possibilities:

1. Rename current methods with a ``resource_`` prefix (or ``_record`` suffix, whatever) and add methods for low-level (get, create, update, delete).
1. Rewrite current backends to perform only low-level operations, and wrap them to provide a resource storage backend like the current one
1. Add another group of backends implementation (e.g. leveldb) (in addition to cache and storage) to provide low-level storage (with a schema similar to cache)
1. Got to go to eat right now.

Currently, the document type manager, that may be included in `websubmit` or be called from the detailed page allows to set a document type per file uploaded. It also displays the file types, and a description, however the `bibdoc` document type is not easily visible which may cause trouble if more than one document is attached like this:

![doctype-def](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7702850/31808492-ad86882c-b573-11e7-83f7-f6fd15356a85.png)

At join2 publication workflows for OpenAccess require certain doc types to be used for release to the public while other doc types are never released by our processes. E.g. in the above procedures would release the handout, the slides, both doc type `Main` but not the source Doc Type: `LaTeX`.

Unfortunately, the current system gives no easy access of the doc type to our librarians who have to check the legal situation before anything is released at all and if a user happens to add only doc types that can not be released they would need to tackle this situation as well.

To streamline the workflow join² suggests to extend the display of doc file manager (used in join² in `websubmit`) by displaying the internal doc type:

# Problem

Related to RFC #2856.

Currently we have the following commit message directives (see [here](http://invenio.readthedocs.org/en/latest/developers/git-workflow.html#r2b-commit-message-qa-review-directives)):

```
Signed-off-by
Reported-by
Co-authored-by
Improved-by
Acked-by
Tested-by
Reviewed-by
```

You can broadly categorise them in two types:
* directives related to giving credit (co-authored-by, reported-by)
* directives related to quality stamping (reviewed-by, signed-off-by)

For new contributors it's not easy to know which to use when, and additionally it's not clear if the differences in signatures is actually used.

# Proposal

Simplify the possible commit directives to:

* ``Signed-off-by`` - (Related to quality) Used by authors, testers and reviewers to sign that a given commit adheres to Invenio's quality standards and to the best of their knowledge is ready to be merged. 
* ``Co-autored-by`` - (Related to credit) Used to acknowledge significant co-authorship including when a person significantly improves the code after the original author left.

and exceptionally:

* ``Reported-by`` - Use only in exceptional circumstances, when there's no GitHub issue (could happen when an external user reports a bug report via email, and not issue was created).

Note: Tester/reviewers should only sign commits they can fully confirm the quality off. Blanco signing of commits my negatively impact their merit and credibility. For commits to be **considered** for integration a minimum of two ``Signed-off-by`` signatures are needed. That a commit is considered for integration, does not mean the commit actually will be integrated.

The following cookiecutter change:

   https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98

should be propagated to this Invenio module.

Namely, in `run-tests.sh`, the sphinx for doctests is invoked after pytest run:

```console
$ tail -3 ./\{\{\ cookiecutter.project_shortname\ \}\}/run-tests.sh
sphinx-build -qnNW docs docs/_build/html && python setup.py test && sphinx-build -qnNW -b doctest docs docs/_build/doctest
```

This sometimes led to problems on Travis CI with the second sphinx-build run due
to "disappearing" dependencies after the example application was tested.

A solution that worked for invenio-marc21 (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49#issuecomment-281287103)
and that was integrated in cookiecutter (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98) was to
run doctest execution in pytest, removing the second `sphinx-build` invocation.

This both solved Travis CI build failures and simplified test suite execution.

Note that this change may necessitate to amend the code tests etc so that things
would be executed with the Flask application context (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49/commits/09e98fc18fd5591dc65d806286e8da51b7e384b4).
Create basic templates for MARC21 compatible records in `examples/templates/invenio_marc21/detail.html` that can be later moved to new `invenio-marc21` package.

issue_title: Pointers outside the heap not correctly caught
issue_body:
An item with a pointer that is beyond the end of the heap will update min_length, but not heap_length. Since is_complete does not check min_length, a malformed packet could cause a dangling pointer and a crash (or security problem).

The simplest fix is probably to make is_complete require min_length to also match heap_length, which will cause such malformed heaps to never be complete.

Below is what I know for now:
let's say `/hello` is the normal endpoint and `/jnius` is the endpoint which will invoke jar package code. pyjnius setup is executed before the flask `app.run()`.
1. Using Flask alone or Flask+uwsgi+nginx or Flask+nginx to host, sending requests to endpoint `/hello`, all the things are fine. Also ok if I use `jpype` instead of `pyjnius`
2. Using Flask alone or Flask+nginx, pyjnius works fine when I use 100 threads to send 30k requests to endpoint `/jnius`. the whole flask app will crash if use `jpype` instead of `pyjnius`.
3. Using Flask+uwsgi+nginx, the whole application will hang up after handling about 100+ requests to endpoint `jnius`, and the following request to any endpoint will also hang up to timeout. 

Any idea about the explanation?

This app is used in the production environment, I can either use Flask alone (this will heavily decrease the throughout the app could handle which is not ok for production environment) or try to write another java program to handle the things about jar package and communicate with python code.

The variable `OMP_NUM_THREADS` is somewhat the gold standard to configure the thread count of an application. Similar to tools like OpenBlas, numexpr should support this variable as a fallback method to its custom variable `NUMEXPR_NUM_THREADS`. 

By supporting  `OMP_NUM_THREADS` it is easier for operators to control all libaries and applications at once.

issue_title: UnicodeDecodeError while SanitizePasswordsProcessor sanitize str keys in Py2
issue_body:
Hi all!

I have problem with this line inside `raven/processors.py:SanitizePasswordsProcessor:sanitize`:
```
key = text_type(key).lower()
```

I've got `UnicodeDecodeError`, because my key value can contain non-ascii symbols. I think it's absolutely correct, because I have a dictionary, where Django models are keys. And when this dictionary serialized to value into frames, models calls `__repr__` method, which return `str`.

That's why I think before `text_type(key)` should be explicit decode for Py2 string. I'll attach pull request later today, if you don't mind!

Thanks for your attention!

When doing
```
ans = x(select=mean(f.v1), groupby="id1")
```
we are getting aggregated results that are also sorted by `id1`. It is sometimes useful to retain original order of values from `id1` column, so aggregated results does not have to be sorted.

Connexion has a hard dependency on `typing` which is useless when running the latest version of Python.

Collecting typing>=3.6.1 (from connexion)

### Steps to reproduce

Install Connexion with Python 3.6.1.

### Additional info:

Python 3.6.1 on MacOS
Connexion Version: 1.1.10

issue_title: Support for NEXRAD Level 3 product 176, Digital Instantaneous Precipitation Rate (DPR)
issue_body:
Py-ART cannot read NEXRAD Level 3 Digital Instantaneous Precipitation Rate (DPR) files (product 176).  They are stored as the `Generic Radial Product Format` which is not supported by the nexrad_level3.py module.

This product stores data in Generic Data Package packets (packet code 28) which is defined in Figure 3-15c on page 3-132 of "INTERFACE CONTROL DOCUMENT FOR THE RPG TO CLASS 1 USER" RPG Build 13.0, Document Number 2620001T.  This packet serializes the data using the External Data Representation (XDR) format, see Appendix E of the above document and RFC 1832.  

Reading this data should be possible with the [**xdrlib** module in the standard library](https://docs.python.org/3/library/xdrlib.html)
User can override SplitTestModel's `execute` method

#### Problem description

Namespaces/ Identifiers may change over time/ become deprecated! Warn the user for this possibility => False positives!

> FYI, here is an example of a KEGG ID that was given in a previous model (iMK1208) but is now obsolete. By matching reaction IDs in MetaCyc we could still find the new KEGG compound ID. Of course Memote isn't expected to check these kind of things, but good to be aware of this when you write some documentation.
- Eduard Kerkhoven

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8027271/35000854-b98cfcde-fae5-11e7-8cd4-cb0d6b213f60.png)


This would fall right through since both match the `kegg.compund` regex

issue_title: [checkoutlinesufo] Hash is stale on first run when writing to default layer
issue_body:
Later in the workflow this bug will cause `makeotf` to fail building fonts, emitting messages like this:
`src glyph Aacute is newer than processed layer. programName: CheckOutlines`

Steps to reproduce the problem:
1. Start with a UFO without a **processedHashMap** file. The UFO must contain a glyph that has overlaps or components.
2. Run the command `checkoutlinesufo -e -w -q font.ufo`

**Result:** Glyphs that had overlaps and/or components will now be flat; file processedHashMap will be created but the hashes of those glyphs are stale, as they represent the glyphs' original state.

**Workaround:** Run the command again; this will update those glyphs' hash.

I'll add a test case for this once the `integration-tests` branch is merged.

issue_title: _PropertyMixin.patch() does not work as expected in a batch
issue_body:
One big takeaway is that `connection` should not be bound to `Blob` and `Bucket` (#728).

----------

If the `Bucket` does not explicitly have the `batch` set as it's connection, the request happens outside the batch:

```python
>>> from gcloud import storage
>>> from gcloud.storage.batch import Batch
>>> storage._PROJECT_ENV_VAR_NAME = 'GCLOUD_TESTS_PROJECT_ID'
>>> storage.set_defaults()
>>> bucket_name = 'dsmlmsldfsacjnajdnkewee'
>>> connection = storage.get_default_connection()
>>> bucket = storage.Bucket(name=bucket_name, connection=connection)
>>> blob = storage.Blob('foo', bucket=bucket)
>>> blob._reload_properties()
<Blob: dsmlmsldfsacjnajdnkewee, foo>
>>> blob.content_type = 'foo/bar'
>>>
>>> with Batch() as batch:
...     blob.patch()
...
<Blob: dsmlmsldfsacjnajdnkewee, foo>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 2, in <module>
  File "gcloud/storage/batch.py", line 165, in __exit__
    self.finish()
  File "gcloud/storage/batch.py", line 130, in finish
    raise ValueError("No deferred requests")
ValueError: No deferred requests
```

On the other hand, if we do the setup correctly, it puts the `blob` in an undefined state (I [referenced][1] this some time ago):

```python
>>> from gcloud import storage
>>> from gcloud.storage.batch import Batch
>>> storage._PROJECT_ENV_VAR_NAME = 'GCLOUD_TESTS_PROJECT_ID'
>>> storage.set_defaults()
>>>
>>> with Batch() as batch:
...     bucket_name = 'dsmlmsldfsacjnajdnkewee'
...     bucket = storage.Bucket(name=bucket_name, connection=batch)
...     blob = storage.Blob('foo', bucket=bucket)
...     blob.content_type = 'foo/bar'
...     blob.patch()
...
<Blob: dsmlmsldfsacjnajdnkewee, foo>
>>> blob._properties
''
>>> blob.content_type
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "gcloud/storage/_helpers.py", line 163, in _getter
    return self.properties[fieldname]
  File "gcloud/storage/_helpers.py", line 64, in properties
    return self._properties.copy()
AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'copy'
```

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/pull/683#issuecomment-76275986

issue_title: Should language document_from_text() etc. by a reference to the Document class
issue_body:
See https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/pull/2062/files/d36ad4db94666450786d4f667aea0607ff2113cb#r76032786

The question is whether we should have `Client.document_from_foo` methods or just have `Client.document` be a reference to the class `Document` and then use `Client.document.from_foo`.

This amounts to swapping `_` for `.` (i.e. no less typing) and makes a "proper noun" like `Document` be referenced as an "improper noun" as a property, which is not a win IMO

issue_body: Typo causing problems with Tasks API.

Hello,
I was using cloud speech and therefore reading the docs when I came across a problem:
```python
operation = client.long_running_recognize(audio, config)
```
it returns an _OperationFuture object from python Gax Library. Here is the [source code](http://gax-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_modules/google/gax.html?highlight=_operation).
But the attributes the documentation tells this object has are untrue. Indeed operation.complete really is operation.done().
And operation.poll() really is operation._poll() but isn't necessary here because operation.results became the method operation.result() which blocks untils operation.complete() is True.
The documentation that needs an update is here:
https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/speech/index.html

Kind regards :dango:

issue_title: should dill.source get dynamically added attributes?
issue_body:
```
>>> import dill
>>> class Foo(object):
...   pass
... 
>>> def bar(self, x):
...   return x**2
... 
>>> Foo.bar = bar
>>> 
>>> print dill.source.getsource(Foo)
class Foo(object):
  pass
>>> print dill.source.getsource(Foo.bar)
def bar(self, x):
  return x**2
```

This might require inspecting the `__dict__` of `Foo`, and finding the objects that don't come from the parent and aren't named in the source.  However, this is tricky… because, it also then requires a grab of all source for all objects dynamically added.  Rather than a merge into the original code object, it should probably return something like this:
```
class Foo(object):
  pass

def bar(self, x):
  return x**2

Foo.bar = bar
```

…which runs the risk of polluting/corrupting namespaces.

I am calling `dill.dump(obj, file)`, where `obj` is a very large python `dict` of `dicts` and `file` is an open file handle, and receive the following crashlog (I am using `python 3.4.3` and `dill 0.2.5`):

```
File "/lustre/scratch/inf/thk22/code/apt-research/src/ukwac_preprocessing/preprocess.py", line 254, in cache_filter_concatenation
    vector_utils.save_vector_cache(ukwac_vectors, vector_out_file=filtered_vector_file)
  File "/lustre/scratch/inf/thk22/code/apt-toolkit/apt_toolkit/utils/vector_utils.py", line 232, in save_vector_cache
    dill.dump(vectors, data_file)
  File "/mnt/nfs2/inf/thk22/.virtualenvs/nlp/lib/python3.4/site-packages/dill/dill.py", line 238, in dump
    pik.dump(obj)
  File "/cm/shared/apps/python/3.4.3/lib/python3.4/pickle.py", line 412, in dump
    self.save(obj)
  File "/cm/shared/apps/python/3.4.3/lib/python3.4/pickle.py", line 479, in save
    f(self, obj) # Call unbound method with explicit self
  File "/mnt/nfs2/inf/thk22/.virtualenvs/nlp/lib/python3.4/site-packages/dill/dill.py", line 837, in save_module_dict
    and obj is getattr(_import_module(obj['__name__'],True), '__dict__', None):
  File "/mnt/nfs2/inf/thk22/.virtualenvs/nlp/lib/python3.4/site-packages/dill/dill.py", line 729, in _import_module
    return __import__(import_name)
TypeError: __import__() argument 1 must be str, not dict
```

I traced down this error a little bit and the problematic line is: ` elif '__name__' in obj and obj != _main_module.__dict__ and obj is getattr(_import_module(obj['__name__'],True), '__dict__', None):` (line 822-823 in dill.py, line numbers are different in the crashlog because i've added a bunch of log statements throughout the file for debugging), the actual crash happens in the fn `_import_module()`. However the problem is that `__name__` happens to be a key in my `dict` and thus the wrong branch is executed in `save_module_dict()` with `_import_module()` not receiving the `str` it expects (but a `dict`). I can't really change the name of the key or remove it (at least not at that processing stage) as the `dict` contains word counts over a large NLP corpus.

I am well aware that this issue is probably an absolute border case, however I would be grateful for some suggestions as to how I can workaround the problem in my local `dill` copy without breaking any of the functionality.

Should dill take 9x as much time as cPickle here? Is there anything we can be doing to speed this up?

Thanks!

```python
obj=['{}'.format(n) for n in range(50000)]
obj

import dill
%timeit len(dill.dumps(obj))
#100 loops, best of 3: 233 ms per loop

import cPickle
%timeit len(cPickle.dumps(obj))

```

issue_title: Multiprocess Failing to pickle with pyOpenSSL version 16
issue_body:

I've written a wrapper around Gspread (which is itself a wrapper, I know) and am also using Google-api-python-client to interface with Google Drive for more generic drive operations.

Part of my goals with the wrapper is to set up process monitoring - so that calls to the Google Drive API can timeout or otherwise fail without halting the whole program. issue_title: Unable to use CreationPolicy on supported types
issue_body:
According to CFN documentation http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-attribute-creationpolicy.html the CreationPolicy attribute is supported on AutoScalingGroup, Instance and WaitCondition.  Currently none of these types include CreationPolicy in their attributes.

Additionally both CreationPolicy and UpdatePolicy currently inherit from BaseAWSObject but based on the AWS documentation they are intended to be used as attributes.  To make things work correctly I believe these should inherit from AWSProperty instead.

issue_title: Support segregation of `resolve.npm` results by arch
issue_body:
As @huckphin pointed out, right now Medium has a huge time sink problem with plain-old-npm since npm does not appear to bake arch info into native packages.  This means that builds on dev machines - say OS X - cannot share resolve results with CI machines or production - say Linux.  Pants should provide the option or always segregate resolve results by architechture.  IOW the arch should be part of the NpmResolve task's cache key.  At worst this is slower for pure js builds where segregation was not needed, but with the feature, expensive resolves can be shared via the distributed cache.

Not sure if @chrispesto has run into this, but I'm guessing this is a feature Twitter would also leverage to allow safe remote artifact cache sharing.

issue_title: `./pants roots` does not respect `source` scope options.
issue_body:
As found in #3417 and demonstratable in https://github.com/pantsbuild/issues-3417, [custom source root configuration](https://github.com/pantsbuild/issues-3417/blob/master/pants.ini#L13-L19) like so:
```ini
[source]
# TODO(John Sirois): Eliminate these hand-specified roots once this issue is resolved:
#  https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/2483
source_roots: {
    '3rdparty/go': ['go_remote'],
    'src/go/src': ['go']
  }
```

Works:
```
$ ./pants options | grep source_roots
source.source_roots = {u'src/go/src': [u'go'], u'3rdparty/go': [u'go_remote']} (from CONFIG in pants.ini)
```

But `./pants roots` does not see this:
```
$ ./pants roots
src/go: go
3rdparty/go: go_remote
```
The reported `src/go` `go` root is actually configured to be `src/go/src`.

issue_title: Give local process_execution its own configurable execution pools
issue_body:
This code https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/b9d3ef73061493a4186fd28593b9e27da5ec4db6/src/rust/engine/src/nodes.rs#L557-L573 currently uses the CPU Pool which we use to rate-limit filesystem operations to perform process execution. This shouldn't be the case - it should have its own pool for rate limiting process execution. Instead, we should:

1. Extract a struct named `CommandRunner` or something similar in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/b9d3ef73061493a4186fd28593b9e27da5ec4db6/src/rust/engine/process_execution/src/local.rs which `run_command_locally` lives on.
2. Give `Core` (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/b9d3ef73061493a4186fd28593b9e27da5ec4db6/src/rust/engine/src/context.rs#L22-L30) an instance of that struct, and modify https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/b9d3ef73061493a4186fd28593b9e27da5ec4db6/src/rust/engine/src/nodes.rs#L557-L573 to use that struct instance instead of calling the static `run_command_locally` method.
3. Rename `Core`'s existing `pool` to `fs_pool` or similar.
4. Give the `CommandRunner` struct a `Arc<ResettablePool>` field, have the `spawn`ing that currently happens in nodes.rs instead happen in in the `CommandRunner` method. Make `run_command_locally` return a `BoxFuture` instead of a `Result`.
5. Give the `CommandRunner` a `reset_prefork` method, which resets the `ResettablePool`.
6. Pass a `ResettablePool` into the `CommandRunner`, either making one in its `new` method or making one in `Core`'s `new` method and passing it in.
7. In `Core`'s `pre_fork`, reset the `CommandRunner` just like we reset other things there.
8. If it's not obvious why the `pre_fork` stuff is happening, add some comments, possibly gleaning some of the existing comments which exist in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/rust/engine/fs/src/pool.rs#L9-L17 

For bonus points, we should make the size of the pool configurable from an option in the Python code, but let's tackle that as a follow-up :)

If your org is thinking about switching to pants and you're doing some experiments, what are some signs that Pants is a good fit? The first_concepts doc kinda touches on this by saying what Pants optimizes for.

Maybe we should have a section about this? It came up in conversation, which suggests folks are curious.

+ Pants' caching speeds things up. Pants should be faster than whatever tool you're using, esp if built-things are already cached. (We say this with confidence if you use Scala; partly because there aren't many other choices to compare with. I ?guess? we're not so certain about other languages?)

+ Pexes are sweet; Pants is a sweet way to build Pex

issue_body:
Running `kolibri -h` does not mention the `--webpack` and `--karma` flags.

It also says that everything following the `--` are "passed on to the django manage command", but this isn't true for those flags.

## Summary

I think committing to the "tree structure" [data model](http://kolibri.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dev/content/concepts_and_definitions.html) will lead to significant limitation for content reuse. The problem is discussed below, and an alternative is proposed.


## Scenario
Consider the following use case:

  - author creates content node `quadratic equation` which is a summary of how to use the quadratic equation, that consists of three subnodes:
    ```
    quadratic_eqns = ContentNode(title='Quadratic equations')
    intro = ContentNode(title='Intro paragraph', parent_id=quadratic_eqns.id)
    formulas = ContentNode(title='Quadratic formula', parent_id=quadratic_eqns.id)
    proof = ContentNode(title='Derivation of the q. eqn', parent_id=quadratic_eqns.id)
    extra = ContentNode(title='Extra explanations', parent_id=quadratic_eqns.id)
    ```
  - author creates two tutorials that include the `quadratic_eqns`
     - Basic math techniques
       - math topic 1
       - math topic 2
       - solving quadratic equations
       - math topic n
     - Solving kinematics problems
       - phys topic 1
       - phys topic 2
       - solving quadratic equations
       - phys topic n

Using the current data model (where each `ContentNode` has a unique `parent_id`) it would be impossible for the ContentNode `quadratic_eqns` to be used in both places.

Furthermore, if the current data model is somehow modified to allow `ContentNode`s  to be reused in multiple locations another problem would arise if the author chooses to customize the nodes that will be included as part of the two tutorials.

Assume the author wants to include different subsets of the `ContentNode`s from the `quadratic_eqns`. The author wants the first tutorial (Basic math techniques) to include the full contents: `[intro,formulas,proof,extra]`, whereas the second tutorial (Solving kinematics problems) should include just the summary `[intro,formulas]`.

The author will have to "fork" `quadratic_eqns` ContentNode and make two versions, one with the full contents and one with the reduced contents.

## Unnecessary coupling of concerns
It seems there is unnecessary coupling between the tree-like structure needed for presentation:

   - `parent_id`
   - `sort_order`
   - `lft`
   - `rght`
   - `tree_id` (not sure what this does)
   - `level`

And the content/metadata needs:

  - `license_id`/`license_owner`
  - `title`
  - `content_id`
  - `description`
  - `author`
  - `kind`
  - `available`
  - `stemmed_metaphone` ??
  - `has_prerequisite`
  - `related`
  - `tags`


## Alternative
Like with all things in software architecture, one possible solution would be to add a level of indirection between `ContentNode` objects and the actual contents.

For example a new model `ContentItem` could be build that focuses on the content/metadata concerns and each `ContentNode` can "point to" a `ContentItem`. This will allow for reuse of `ContentItem`s in multiple contexts.

A useful analogy for thinking about this proposed change is `ContentItems` as songs, and the (hierarchy of) `ContentNode`s as a playlist of pointers to songs.

I have been thinking about "data representation" for educational content for the past 10+ years so I'll be happy to discuss further.

issue_title: Adding placeholder in german causes english to be created
issue_body:
Hello,

When I create an article in german and go to it's detail page, add a text plugin and save, this triggers an insert in the translations  table for english and thus creates the english translation without a title/content without me asking it to do so.

The problem lies [here](https://github.com/aldryn/aldryn-newsblog/blob/master/aldryn_newsblog/models.py#L516), in here we try to save the search_data when a plugin is added to article's content placeholder but it never checks the plugin's language and so it gets the article which in my case comes back in english and saves which causes the english translation to be created.

Seems like an easy fix, here just need to use instance.language to make sure the correct translation is updated.

Here's a video of the issue https://www.dropbox.com/s/yms4s7gdokuhv68/newsblog-pl-issue.webm?dl=0

issue_title: TypeError: load() missing 1 required positional argument: 'data'
issue_body:
I ran the example in README.md and caught this error while POST-ing data to `/user` endpoint with the payload:

```json
{
  "username": "itsjef",
  "password": "itsme"
}
```

It was this line: https://github.com/numberly/flask-stupe/blob/master/flask_stupe/validation.py#L19 that caused the bug, since `schema.load()` was an instance method.

My workaround is to replace

```python
@schema_required(UserSchema)
```

with

```python
@schema_required(UserSchema())
```

Additional Info:
- python version: **3.6.5**
- flask-stupe version: **3.6.4**
- pymongo: **3.7.1**
- marshmallow: **2.15.4**

issue_title: Some examples in the documentation don't work with Python 3
issue_body:
For example this one:

```
>>> from docker import Client
>>> cli = Client(base_url='tcp://127.0.0.1:2375')
>>> for line in cli.pull('busybox', stream=True):
...     print(json.dumps(json.loads(line), indent=4))
```

will produce:

```
TypeError: the JSON object must be str, not 'bytes'

issue_title: Using docker.client.DockerClient.containers.run with auto_remove=True causes a crash
issue_body:
What happens is straightforward: setting auto_remove=True in the arguments to run() causes the container to be removed [before the Python client attempts to read the logs](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/docker/models/containers.py#L734).  This then raises an error like:

```
docker.errors.NotFound: 404 Client Error: Not Found ("No such container: 0e9a9dd8072a1b80ead74bc47466493ffea92eca7acf1c5f3b29ce6faf861161")
```

This is not how executing `docker run -rm` works, where the output still goes to stdout, and it means that calling run() with auto_remove=True isn't useful.

issue_title: Out of memory operations on huge numpy arrays
issue_body:
### Subject of the issue
The size of numpy arrays can get quite huge in `DiscreteFactor` resulting in `MemoryError`. Therefore, we need a way to store these arrays out of memory and do operations on it. 

### Possible Solutions:
1. numpy.memmap :- Stores the array in a binary file and allows reading/writing parts of this array. But we will still need to write a wrapper around this for doing efficient operations on these arrays.

issue_body: Make sure the URL being fed in exists.  Else, we end up with things in queued state.

I may just be missing it, but it doesn't seem to be possible to do the equivalent of `//` in XPath. I.e. find the desired expression anywhere in the supplied JSON versus from the root of it. If not, can that be made a feature request?

The use case is when you are trying to find subsets of JSON in a document whose structure you cannot predict in advance.

Let's say I have this list: `[[1, 2, 3, [4]], [5, 6, 7, [8, 9]]`

I want to extract this output: `[[1, 2, 3, 4], [5, 6, 7, 8, 9]]`

It seems like this query should work: `[*][]`, i.e., "project the input into a list, and flatten each element of the outer list" but it doesn't work. I get `[1, 2, 3, [4], 5, 6, 7, [8, 9]]`, which is the same as if I had just passed in `[]`. Oddly, `[*][0]` does return what I would expect, `[1, 5]` which the first element of each element of the outer list. Why is it that in the `[*][0]` expression the `[0]` operates on each element of the top-level list, while in `[*][]` the `[]` seems to operate on the list as a whole? I would expect that behavior out of `[*] | []`. Similarly, `[0][]` returns `[1, 2, 3, 4]` and `[1][]` returns `[5, 6, 7, 8, 9]`.

I see the same behavior both on the released 0.7.1 and the current develop branch.

Thanks!

--Joel

The documentation goes over the process of inferring which sedes class to use when serializing an object, [on this page](http://pyrlp.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial.html#sedes-inference). It mentions that the function `rlp.infer_sedes` checks `rlp.sedes.sedes_list` to find a sedes object which can handle serializing whatever you are trying to encode. But the code for `infer_sedes` never checks any list like this, and the list `rlp.sedes.sedes_list` doesn't even exist! Not sure why this discrepancy between the code and the docs exist, thought I'd make an issue to point it out though.

### What is wrong?

The `Collation` object currently uses a stateful and mutation based mechanism for storing the total transaction fee.

https://github.com/jannikluhn/py-evm/blob/65d7b24d718b1aa1238ba72a385d16baa93498e7/evm/vm/forks/sharding/collations.py#L29

It would be better if we can find a way to track that value and expose it in the appropriate place(s) in a more explicit way.

### How can it be fixed

Need to investigate.

issue_title: Code duplication in peer handling services
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

As identified [here](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/758#discussion_r190611644), various services (`FastChainSyncer`, `StateDownloader`, `LightPeerChain`, and `ShardSyncer`) implement the same peer handling pattern:

- They subscribe to a peer pool
- They start a `handle_peer` task whenever a new peer registers
- `handle_peer` takes messages from the peer's message queue and processes them depending on the message type
- When the services are stopped they cancel all peer handling tasks

### How can it be fixed

Add a common parent class (`PeerHandlingService`?) that implements that functionality.

Related: #746, #768

### What is this?

Just dumping an idea here.

### The Idea

Tool to do some sort of persistent *mapping* of the p2p network and ideally expose that data in a queryable format.

issue_title: getCode produces "0x" on smart contract addresses
issue_body:
Version: 4.0.0
Python: 3.5
OS: Linux 

### What was wrong?

I have Geth (version 1.7.2) node, which was synced using the "fast" mode. When I use the `getCode` method on smart contract addresses, it always returns "0x". I have verified this with known smart contract addresses like the Iconomi ERC20 token contract address. 

I am wondering why this is happening? Is it possible that nodes synced in "fast" mode cannot return the contract bytecode? Or is it a problem with web3py?

Any help is appreciated.

issue_title: Change subcommand names to be consistent with `wheel`
issue_body:
Wheel's API provides the following:

* wheel.archive
* wheel.install
* wheel.pkginfo
* wheel.util

Maybe we should change the names of the subcommands to:

* wagon archive (instead of create)
* wagon pkginfo (instead of show)

WDYT?

While we can declare that wagon only supports `manylinux1` if the wheels in it are `manylinux1`, I think we should add the ability to convert `linux_x64_86` to `manylinux1` to make the job a bit more comfortable for the user.

WDYT?

In conda < 4.3 the function was simply:
```
def cache_fn_url(url):
    md5 = hashlib.md5(url.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()
    return '%s.json' % (md5[:8],)
```
however, in 4.3, an additional `url = url.rstrip('/')` has been added.  Therefore the resulting filename is different.

This means that updating to conda 4.3.2 will automatically invalidate all existing caches of `repodata` from any channel.  I'm not sure if this is going to be a big problem, but I assume that people who operate in offline mode are going to suddenly not be able to do operations which involve rely on having a local cache of repodata.  I'm thinking about air-gapped environments.  The easiest solution would be to restore the old functionality.

issue_title: Detect cross-filesystem situation once, not on every file write
issue_body:
If I'm reading the output of `--debug` correctly it looks like conda may be checking the "hard link, sym link, copy" situation on every single file inside a package as it goes to install a package.  It seems to me that the decision that is made for the first file will be identical for all the files in the package.  And perhaps all the packages in the command (though this may not be the case, depending on where packages are picked up from).

If I'm working in an enterprise context it is very likely that the location where the `root` Anaconda environment is installed and where I am doing `conda` operations will be across file systems.  Or even across NFS.  In these situations the impact could be substantial if these checks could be done just once per package (or per command) rather than per-file.

issue_title: Getting latest versions of all packages
issue_body:
Hello! Now I'm trying to create a conda environment with all packages I need having their latest versions, and with mkl acceleration if available. For this, I add `conda-forge` channel as some packages are absent from `defaults` (but I give the highest priority to `defaults`), then install all the packages with `conda install` (the list is quite long so I don't give it here, but I guess it doesn't change much), and to ensure that everything has the latest version  I run `conda update --all`.

The output from the `install` command looks reasonable but for these lines:
```
    openblas:                          0.2.19-0                                     
    opencv:                            3.2.0-np113py36_blas_openblas_204 conda-forge [blas_openblas]
    scipy:                             0.19.1-py36_blas_openblas_202     conda-forge [blas_openblas]
```
For some reason it seems to install `opencv` and `scipy` with openblas instead of MKL and I have no idea how to fix it.

Then the mentioned `conda update --all` surprisingly suggests some updates, even when run immediately after installation! For example, it suggests the following actions:

```
The following packages will be UPDATED:

    astropy:      2.0.1-np113py36_0                             --> 2.0.1-np112py36_0             
    cairo:        1.14.6-4                          conda-forge --> 1.14.8-0

    numexpr:      2.6.2-np113py36_0                             --> 2.6.2-np112py36_0             
    pyfftw:       0.10.4-np113py36_2                conda-forge --> 0.10.4-np112py36_2 conda-forge
    pywavelets:   0.5.2-np113py36_0                             --> 0.5.2-np112py36_0             
    qt:           5.6.2-3                           conda-forge --> 5.6.2-5                       
    scikit-image: 0.13.0-np113py36_0                            --> 0.13.0-np112py36_0            
    scikit-learn: 0.19.0-np113py36_0                            --> 0.19.0-np112py36_0            
    statsmodels:  0.8.0-np113py36_0                             --> 0.8.0-np112py36_0             

The following packages will be SUPERSEDED by a higher-priority channel:

    fontconfig:   2.12.1-4                          conda-forge --> 2.12.1-3                      
    freetype:     2.7-1                             conda-forge --> 2.5.5-2                       
    glib:         2.51.4-0                          conda-forge --> 2.50.2-1                      
    h5py:         2.7.0-np113py36_1                 conda-forge --> 2.7.0-np112py36_0             
    harfbuzz:     1.3.4-2                           conda-forge --> 0.9.39-2                      
    hdf5:         1.8.18-0                          conda-forge --> 1.8.17-2                      
    icu:          58.1-1                            conda-forge --> 54.1-0                        
    libpng:       1.6.28-0                          conda-forge --> 1.6.27-0                      
    matplotlib:   2.0.2-py36_2                      conda-forge --> 2.0.2-np112py36_0             
    pango:        1.40.3-3                          conda-forge --> 1.40.3-1                      
    pillow:       4.2.1-py36_0                      conda-forge --> 4.2.1-py36_0                  
    pytables:     3.4.2-np113py36_1                 conda-forge --> 3.4.2-np112py36_0             
    scipy:        0.19.1-py36_blas_openblas_202     conda-forge [blas_openblas] --> 0.19.1-np112py36_0            

The following packages will be DOWNGRADED:

    numpy:        1.13.1-py36_0                                 --> 1.12.1-py36_0                 
    opencv:       3.2.0-np113py36_blas_openblas_204 conda-forge [blas_openblas] --> 3.1.0-np112py36_1  conda-forge
```
Which is very confusing and even includes `numpy` downgrade.

If I try to run `conda update --all --no-channel-priority`, it suggests another set of actions:
```
    cryptography:       1.8.1-py36_0                              --> 1.9-py36_0         conda-forge
    dbus:               1.10.20-0                                 --> 1.10.22-0          conda-forge

    keras:              2.0.5-py36_0                              --> 2.0.6-py36_0       conda-forge
    libpq:              9.5.4-0                                   --> 9.6.3-0            conda-forge
    libtool:            2.4.2-0                                   --> 2.4.6-0            conda-forge
    mako:               1.0.6-py36_0                              --> 1.0.7-py36_0       conda-forge
    protobuf:           3.2.0-py36_0                              --> 3.3.2-py36_0       conda-forge
    psycopg2:           2.7.1-py36_0                              --> 2.7.3-py36_0       conda-forge
    pymc3:              3.0-py36_0                                --> 3.1-py36_0         conda-forge
    pyopenssl:          17.0.0-py36_0                             --> 17.2.0-py36_0      conda-forge
    pyzmq:              16.0.2-py36_0                             --> 16.0.2-py36_2      conda-forge
    requests:           2.14.2-py36_0                             --> 2.18.4-py36_1      conda-forge
    setuptools:         27.2.0-py36_0                             --> 36.2.2-py36_0      conda-forge
    widgetsnbextension: 2.0.0-py36_0                              --> 3.0.0-py36_0       conda-forge
    zeromq:             4.1.5-0                                   --> 4.2.1-1            conda-forge
```
Which makes more sense, but anyway I don't understand why not install these versions when I run `install`. Even when running `install` with `--no-channel-priority` the consequent `update` still suggests significant actions.

Any suggestions on how to get all latest package versions & mkl acceleration where it's available?

issue_title: Automatically set ca_certs if certifi if installed and verify_certs=True
issue_body:
At the moment to use SSL certificate verification with the urllib3 backend, [the docs](https://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.io/en/master/index.html#ssl-and-authentication) say to:
* pass `verify=True`
* either set `ca_certs` to the location of the system root certificates, or else install certifi and use:
```python
import certifi

es = Elasticsearch(
    # ...
    verify_certs=True,
    ca_certs=certifi.where(),
)
```

Ideally it would be better to automatically use the value from `certifi.where()` if certifi is available and the user hadn't set `ca_certs` themselves.

This would be similar to the requests library behaviour (except instead of falling back to the vendored version, it would fall back to `None`):
https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/v2.10.0/requests/certs.py#L17

The elasticsearch-py docs could then be simplified to say something like:
*"To enable certificate verification, install certifi and pass `verify_certs=True`"*
...avoiding the need for the `import certifi` and `ca_certs=certifi.where()` boilerplate.

This could also pave the way in the future to inverting the value for `verify_certs` and actually having it enabled by default (or at least by default iff certifi is found).

I'm happy to open a PR if this would be accepted?

issue_title: helpers._chunk_actions produces chunks bigger than max_chunk_bytes
issue_body:
Hello! Thanks for the library.

We started to get ocasional errors from Elasticsearch
```
org.jboss.netty.handler.codec.frame.TooLongFrameException: HTTP content length exceeded 104857600 bytes.
```
So I investigated and found an issue in `helpers._chunk_actions`
There are two cases

1. If `max_chunk_bytes` is pretty low, `_chunk_actions` will split actions one by one, but each chunk will be bigger than `max_chunk_bytes`. It's actually used in test https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/64202273e02ab5f0b99271771d4e8143b3599c3e/test_elasticsearch/test_helpers.py#L35 Maybe it makes sense to raise a warning in this case. 
2. **Most important** `_chunk_actions` count characters instead of bytes to check for chunk size. If user passes actions with unicode values it is possible that output chunk size will exceed `max_chunk_bytes`. This test failes
```python
class TestChunkActions(TestCase):
    def setUp(self):
        super(TestChunkActions, self).setUp()
        self.actions = [({'index': {}}, {'some': u'€€€€€€€€', 'i': i}) for i in range(100)]

    def test_chunks_are_chopped_by_byte_size_properly(self):
        chunks = list(helpers._chunk_actions(self.actions, 100000, 90, JSONSerializer()))
        self.assertEquals(100, len(chunks))
        for chunk in chunks:
            chunk = u''.join(chunk)
            chunk = chunk if isinstance(chunk, str) else chunk.encode('utf-8')
            self.assertLessEqual(len(chunk), 90)
```
`_chunk_actions` should produce 100 chunks (each less than 90 bytes), but produces 50 chunks (each ~114 bytes)

It would appear that `TasksClient` is out of sync with the ES 5.4 `/_tasks` API:

>>> es.tasks.list(nodes='CWVKYew7TEuDxUz3IAsfMw')
TypeError: list() got an unexpected keyword argument 'nodes'```

issue_title: az acr build should exclude .git folder
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
If the source code folder has the vcs folders eg `.git` or `.bzr` , az acr build should exclude the files from the folders in the uploaded tar file.

**To Reproduce**
Source code folder structure:
.git 
 | -- file1
Dockerfile

az acr build -t <registry> <source_code_folder>

**Expected behavior**
The uploaded tar file should exlude `.git` folder. The tar file should only have `Dockerfile`.

**Additional context**
It's a regression from #6950 

Related issue: https://github.com/Azure/acr/issues/122

If users manually add .git in .dockerignore file. The .git folder will be excluded.

issue_title: Azure CLI Commands Hang - Possibly due to telemetry_upload.py
issue_body:
I am currently running docker containers that execute azure cli commands. Some of the azure cli commands involve "az group create" / "az group deploy" / "az vm create" / "az vm extension set" etc. It is not all the time, but these commands hang at times. 

Delving deeper into the processes that are being run, I found many such following processes by running htop.
```
113 jenkins    20   0 93708 18324  3152 S  0.0  0.1  0:00.38 /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/azure/cli/core/telemetry_upload.pyc

These processes run for hours at times. When I kill these processes, my scripts and commands continue so I believe there is some connection to my scripts hanging due to these telemetry_upload calls.

issue_title: Delimiter Support for file and folder upload
issue_body:
This is a nice to have enhancement that allows for record boundaries to be honored during upload, so that no data is uploaded in the middle of a record.

Work required for this:
1. Add functionality to properly chunk large files on delimiter terminations, so that no records span chunks
2. Add awareness of all chunks to the delimiter searching logic to allow for robust error handling and validation.
3. Add E2E tests.

Cost:
~40 hours.

issue_title: Tweak conform to allow ESRI FileGDB sources
issue_body: Indiana's [statewide address source](http://data.openaddresses.io/runs/1436163265.620/us-in.txt) is a FileGDB. OGR [should support it](https://pcjericks.github.io/py-gdalogr-cookbook/vector_layers.html#get-all-layers-in-an-esri-file-geodatabase) with few (if any) changes to the existing code that uses OGR to convert to CSV. We just need to include .gdb in the list of file endings to search for when considering what to send to OGR. We might also want to include a "layer" attribute in the `conform` object so we can be explicit about which GDB layer to extract.

issue_title: Pre-push hook contract is different from git's pre-push contract
Feature request: pre-commit or pre-push only hooks
issue_body:
I am writing a pre-commit hook that validates the author and committer emails of commits. However, these values can only be guaranteed to queried properly *after* the commit is created.

git does not provide utilities to retrieve this information in the pre-commit stage nor can you retrieve explicit information from the --author flag. (http://stackoverflow.com/questions/9627198/stop-a-git-commit-by-a-specific-author-using-pre-commit-hook)

I would like to ensure that this hook is only run at pre-push time. I can think of two implementations:

* yaml config file can specify pre-push or pre-commit only
* pre-push/pre-commit stage is made available via environmental variable when hook is run.

## Long story short

class `DynamicResource` can't be subclassed because (part of) its constructor logic is in `UrlDispatcher.add_resource()`

## Expected behaviour

As a developer, when implementing custom dynamic resource types, I expect it to be possible to derive my new resource type from DynamicResource. I expect the constructors of `DynamicResource` and `PlainResource` to have the same signature

    def __init__(self, path, *, name=None)

where `PlainResource` takes a plain path, and `DynamicResource` takes a templated path.

## Actual behaviour

But when I do so, I find out that the constructor of DynamicResource takes two undocumented parameters (`pattern` and `formatter`) that are very tightly bound to how `UrlDispatcher.add_resource()` constructs either a `PlainResource` or `DynamicResource`.

## Solution

Move constructor logic of class `DynamicResource` from `UrlDispatcher` to where it belongs: `DynamicResource.__init__()`.

I'll submit a PR for this.

Looks like people want for already merged PRs publishing and we have pretty long list of them.

@fafhrd91 ?

Cancelling a web handler task on client disconnection is the most unexpected and annoying feature of aiohttp web server.
People from classic WSGI world don't expect it. 
Moreover tornado doesn't support a cancellation (I hope it will in 5.0 release).
 
The proposal is:
* *web-handler* cancellation should be opt-in, not opt-out (by aiojobs and async_armor usage).
* Application should have *allow_cancel* constructor parameter, `False` by default.
* Any record in application route table should accept *allow_cancel* to override the default.

Opinions?

issue_title: graceful shutdown -- wait for all jobs to finish
issue_body:
I am using aiohttp, and I am using spawn to execute some atomic process in the background. I wonder how can I wait for all jobs to finish before I shutdown the aiohttp server.

Currently, I am using the following code:
```py
async def shutdown(app):
    jobs = get_scheduler_from_app(app)._jobs
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(0)
        if len(jobs) == 0:
            return

app.on_shutdown.append(shutdown)
```

I am not sure wether it's a good/correct way to do that. I hope there will be a method like `scheduler.wait_for_close()` to do it.

PS: I didn't set the limit or the pending limit for the scheduler yet, I know there is a job queue and a pending queue, I'm not sure wether that will affect my code above.

issue_title: Pool.maxsize = 0 does not allow unlimited connections
issue_body:
As noted in docs (https://github.com/aio-libs/aiomysql/blob/master/docs/pool.rst#L60):

    If maxsize is 0 than size of pool is unlimited

A pool with maxsize = 0 ends up hanging on `_acquire` and never yielding a connection.

(aiomysql==0.0.9, Python 3.5.1)

Linux RHEL 6.7 within companies set of VM, latest Jupyter as from "conda update --all", Python 3.5, and Python 3.5 kernel.

Yesterday when suddenly whenever I open or create a new notebook, Jupyter stuck/hung. In the console log, all I can see is these...:
```
[D 10:58:55.114 NotebookApp] Found kernel python3 in /opt/logs/sandbox/conda/share/jupyter/kernels
[D 10:58:55.128 NotebookApp] 304 GET /api/kernelspecs (x.x.x.x) 38.19ms

**<after 7 minutes>**

[D 11:06:24.130 NotebookApp] 200 GET /api/contents/2016-10-27-consumer.ipynb?type=notebook&_=1478883534723 (x.x.x.x) 449000.68ms
...
[D 11:06:25.168 NotebookApp] Starting kernel: ['/opt/logs/sandbox/conda/bin/python', '-m', 'ipykernel', '-f', '/home/xxx/.local/share/jupyter/runtime/kernel-d6bd7421-7664-46a1-86eb-39d2ac515966.json']
[D 11:06:25.175 NotebookApp] Connecting to: tcp://127.0.0.1:55392
[I 11:06:25.176 NotebookApp] Kernel started: d6bd7421-7664-46a1-86eb-39d2ac515966

# Then finally shows up until 7.5 minutes.
# When executing a cell:
# nothing for 6 minutes, then....
[D 11:14:39.551 NotebookApp] 200 PUT /api/contents/2016-10-27-consumer.ipynb (x.x.x.x) 375126.00ms
```

Pretty sure this is related to the environment, possibly some antivirus, iptables, or something behind the scene was implemented automatically as standard policy, I don't have root access so can't find out exactly what was going on. 

Is there a way for me to know more about what /api/contents is actually waiting for to help me know a little more about what was actually going on with tornado/ipython/jupyter?

I just want to know how to use i18n function...
Thanks.

Hi, please provide option for user preference to permanently hide header/toolbar.

From my perspective header is useless to me - it contains Jupyter logo and Notebook title, which I already have in my browser tab. Although it's height is reduced compared to early versions, why waste valuable vertical space?

I made JS start script to auto disable header/toolbar, but with latest version this HTML element is probably changed, and it is not the first time it is changed. So instead digging inside HTML DOM and writing JS, I thought to write to you.

Thanks

issue_title: Is it possible to fold cell editors (or limit the height of editors)? We want to use ipynb as business reports sometimes (they don't like code, but they can read some sql if they want).
issue_body:
a simple way to achieve:
Add a shrink cell mode. In shrink-cell mode, shrink cell height when it is out of focus.

The blue/green 5px left cell border, which we have been styling with gradients is having trouble in master. We saw the same issues with JuptyerLab (we copied the styling). The symptoms are:

* As you resize the browser window, the width of the left border oscillates between 1-6ish px.
* The left border in multiselected mode is up at 7px

We have found a fix in JupyterLab that uses the `:before` pseudoselector to provide a nice clean way of implementing this. Here is the PR on JuptyerLab:

https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/pull/1690

We should port this over to notebook before 5.0 is released.

@gnestor

issue_title: Add 'Clear current cell' to Keyboard shortcuts
issue_body:
I frequently have to delete the output of cells (e.g. if I interrupt a calculation using multiprocessing, which usually leads to a very long error stack) and therefore use the following clickpath:

`Cell --> Current Outputs --> Clear`

It would be great to have a keyboard shortcut to this functionality, since it would speedup something users might do quite frequently. ([It has also been requested on stackoverflow some time ago](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39924826/keyboard-shortcut-to-clear-cell-output-in-jupyter-notebook))
It would be nice if the Python client could upload coverage reports to Teamscale like from Jacoco.

Consider this MWE (`lenet5.h5` is a convolutional neural network in HDF5 file format, built and trained by tf.keras):

issue_title: Write a manuscript for BioConda and BioContainers on Computational Platforms
issue_body:
## BioConda and BioContainers - A Ubiquitous, Lightweight Approach for Reproducible Bioinformatics

### Outline

The problems associated with reproducibility of web services has been well documented [citation needed], so reproducible informatics requires reproducible software installation. There are many approaches to reproducible software installation for informatics - DebianMed, Docker, EasyInstall, software modules, and so on. Many work great in cloud and container environments - where the researcher has full control of a virtual machine or container and may choose whatever software installation mechanism makes sense, but are less appropriate at high performance computing (HPC) centers where large centralized resources make such freedom impossible. On the other hand, the HPC centric approaches don't provide options such as ready-to-run software containers ideal for the cloud. Futhermore, some approaches are built to work with command-line scripting while others are built for specific computational platforms such as the Galaxy Tool Shed or Dockstore. This paper outlines an approach that fits all of these scenarios. Combing a popular binary package manager containing thousands of bioinformatics and general purpose scientific software recipes and container environments built using these binaries for various technologies such as Docker, rtk, and Singularity and then making these packages and containers available on command-line and inside computational platforms such as Galaxy and workflow platforms such as Toil we believe we provide a pervasive approach to reproducibility that works regardless of the technologies used.

We will recreate the analysis in PAPER X four different ways. Using customized shell scripts at an HPC facility, an IPython notebook on a Mac OS X laptop, in a Galaxy history on usegalaxy.org, and deployed on Amazon EC 2 with Toil using the Common Workflow Language. We will use the same binaries seamlessly across these environments leveraging the technologies that make the most sense for each environment.

issue_title: Visual Studio 2008 x64 patch for AppVeyor is incomplete for command-line builds
issue_body:
In order to get a working VS2008 x64 compile working, I had to do the following:
````powershell
            # http://scikit-ci-addons.readthedocs.io/en/latest/addons.html#patch-vs2008-py
            C:\Python27\python -m pip install scikit-ci-addons
            C:\Python27\python -m ci_addons appveyor/patch_vs2008
            # http://help.appveyor.com/discussions/kb/38-visual-studio-2008-64-bit-builds
            Copy-Item "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio 9.0\VC\bin\vcvars64.bat" "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio 9.0\VC\bin\amd64\vcvarsamd64.bat"
````

As noted, the process was documented at http://help.appveyor.com/discussions/kb/38-visual-studio-2008-64-bit-builds and the existing patch takes care of the `setup_x64.bat` part of that description.

The difference is visible from https://ci.appveyor.com/project/TBBle/libxml2-win-binaries/build/1.0.66/job/kgdmrkwp20bs8s04 to https://ci.appveyor.com/project/TBBle/libxml2-win-binaries/build/1.0.67/job/pmm5qnga2uqbfs3x after the application of https://github.com/TBBle/libxml2-win-binaries/commit/e55a7a44b044ba7bdbb1276974f116151f7ed36d.

I haven't tested it, but I believe the patch as-packaged works for IDE-based builds, which don't rely on `vcvars.bat` working.

It's also worth noting this entire patch does not appear to be necessary for building Python wheels, as AppVeyor slaves include the [Microsoft Visual C++ Compiler for Python 2.7](https://www.microsoft.com/en-au/download/details.aspx?id=44266) which setuptools 6 can detect and use, removing the need for a Visual Studio 2008 installation.
